title: Life Behind Larry

jokes.text: Well, I do. I'm gonna keep them waiting.
jokes.setup: Arthur says they don't want to keep network executives waiting
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately contradicts with petulant defiance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders character moment - his childish contrarian nature immediately surfaces when told to be cooperative

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, I do. I'm gonna keep them waiting.

jokes.text: I am. I'm going to piss all over them.
jokes.setup: Arthur tells Larry not to 'piss all over' the network's suggestions
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately confirms he will do exactly that
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry defiance - takes Arthur's metaphor and makes it literal threat. Economy and character integration are strong.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I am. I'm going to piss all over them.

jokes.text: Do you think when Johnny Carson picked David Letterman to go after him at 12:30, he went through this shit? Hell, no. Nobody fucked with Johnny.
jokes.setup: Larry comparing himself to Johnny Carson's decision-making process
jokes.punchline: The absurd delusion that he has Carson-level power
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character delusion - Larry comparing himself to Carson reveals his inflated self-image perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Do you think when Johnny Carson picked David Letterman to go after him at 12:30, he went through this shit? Hell, no. Nobody fucked with Johnny.

jokes.text: That's just what America needs—another fucking awards show. Political bullshit. You kiss somebody's ass, and they send your name to the engravers.
jokes.setup: Network exec mentions the American Television Awards
jokes.punchline: Larry's cynical breakdown of how awards work
jokes.explanation: 'Send your name to the engravers' is a perfectly crafted cynical observation about Hollywood phoniness

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That's just what America needs—another fucking awards show. Political bullshit. You kiss somebody's ass, and they send your name to the engravers.

jokes.text: Will they send a limo? Town car. Oh, good. Much less gaudy.
jokes.setup: Larry asks about transportation to awards show he just trashed
jokes.punchline: His immediate concern about appearing 'gaudy' while caring deeply about the status symbol
jokes.explanation: Perfect hypocrisy reveal - Larry cares deeply about the awards he just criticized, but wants to appear above it all

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Will they send a limo? Town car. Oh, good. Much less gaudy.

jokes.text: Bob Saget. Saget—good name. Excellent name, but he's doing some shows. Wouldn't this be too much? Bob is a mule.
jokes.setup: Network suggests Bob Saget as host candidate
jokes.punchline: Arthur's bizarre 'Bob is a mule' assessment as if work ethic were livestock comparison
jokes.explanation: 'Bob is a mule' is wonderfully absurd Hollywood-speak that sounds profound but means nothing

dialogue.speaker: Network Executive
dialogue.line: Bob Saget. Saget—good name. Excellent name, but he's doing some shows. Wouldn't this be too much?

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: Bob is a mule.

jokes.text: You're raiding the cast of Full House, huh? Check into the Olsen twins. I hear, once their parents go to sleep, they're wild.
jokes.setup: Network suggests Dave Coulier after Bob Saget
jokes.punchline: Larry's inappropriate joke about child actors being 'wild'
jokes.explanation: Dark edge typical of Larry's inappropriate observations, though the child actor angle dates it

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You're raiding the cast of Full House, huh? Check into the Olsen twins. I hear, once their parents go to sleep, they're wild.

jokes.text: Really? I'll see if they're interested.
jokes.setup: Larry makes inappropriate joke about Olsen twins
jokes.punchline: Executive's completely straight response as if it's a legitimate suggestion
jokes.explanation: Perfect network executive obliviousness - treating Larry's inappropriate joke as serious business suggestion

dialogue.speaker: Network Executive
dialogue.line: Really? I'll see if they're interested.

jokes.text: Bobcat Goldthwait.
jokes.setup: Larry promises he has someone perfect in mind for the late-night slot
jokes.punchline: His choice is the deliberately unmarketable Bobcat Goldthwait
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and payoff - Larry's terrible choice reveals his complete disconnect from commercial reality

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Bobcat Goldthwait.

jokes.text: Is he the guy who makes the animal noises? I knew you'd say that. That's just one of his colors.
jokes.setup: Network executive immediately identifies Bobcat by his most unmarketable trait
jokes.punchline: Larry's defensive 'that's just one of his colors' as if it's sophisticated artistry
jokes.explanation: 'One of his colors' is beautifully pretentious way to defend obviously problematic comedy style

dialogue.speaker: Network Executive
dialogue.line: Is he the guy who makes the animal noises?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I knew you'd say that. That's just one of his colors.

jokes.text: There's a lot of funny out there, and we're looking for the right kind of funny.
jokes.setup: Larry defending Bobcat's comedy
jokes.punchline: Network's polite but firm rejection via corporate speak
jokes.explanation: Good corporate-speak satire - 'right kind of funny' is perfectly vague and controlling

dialogue.speaker: Network Executive
dialogue.line: There's a lot of funny out there, and we're looking for the right kind of funny.

jokes.text: Let's take a good look at tomcat. Bobcat. Whatever.
jokes.setup: Arthur trying to support Larry's choice
jokes.punchline: He can't even remember Bobcat's name correctly
jokes.explanation: Arthur's casual dismissal of getting the name right shows his lack of genuine investment

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: Let's take a good look at tomcat. Bobcat. Whatever.

jokes.text: Just passing through town, doing my film. Oh. What film? You doing the Coneheads? No. Hans and Franz.
jokes.setup: Kevin Nealon mentions doing a film
jokes.punchline: Larry's assumption it's SNL sketch movies, which were notoriously bad
jokes.explanation: Industry insider joke about SNL's sketch-to-film track record, but fairly predictable

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Just passing through town, doing my film.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh. What film?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: You doing the Coneheads?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: No. Hans and Franz.

jokes.text: Are you Hans or Franz? Franz—Hans. No. Franz. Franz. Carvey's Hans. Franz is the funny one.
jokes.setup: Kevin explains he's doing Hans and Franz movie
jokes.punchline: Kevin's confusion about his own character and claim Franz is funnier
jokes.explanation: Good character confusion moment - Kevin not knowing his own role and arbitrary comedy hierarchy

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Are you Hans or Franz?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Franz—Hans. No. Franz. Franz. Carvey's Hans. Franz is the funny one.

jokes.text: Bobcat. That's funny. Was Gallagher tied up or something?
jokes.setup: Larry tells Kevin the network wants Bobcat for 12:30
jokes.punchline: Kevin's sarcastic suggestion that Gallagher was the alternative
jokes.explanation: Perfectly cutting comparison - Gallagher represents rock-bottom comedy choice, making the Bobcat decision look even worse

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Bobcat. That's funny. Was Gallagher tied up or something?

jokes.text: Gilbert Gottfried called, Bob Saget called, Allan Havey called several times, Louie Anderson, Steven Wright, Robert Klein, and Sinbad. Goddamn hyenas.
jokes.setup: News leaks about the 12:30 slot search
jokes.punchline: Arthur calling the desperate comics 'goddamn hyenas'
jokes.explanation: 'Goddamn hyenas' perfectly captures the feeding frenzy nature of Hollywood opportunism

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: Gilbert Gottfried called, Bob Saget called, Allan Havey called several times, Louie Anderson, Steven Wright, Robert Klein, and Sinbad. Goddamn hyenas.

jokes.text: What, was David Brenner out of the country? David Brenner sent brownies.
jokes.setup: Arthur asks sarcastically if David Brenner was unavailable
jokes.punchline: Brenner actually did respond, with brownies
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - the brownies detail is absurdly specific and reveals desperate Hollywood schmoozing

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: What, was David Brenner out of the country?

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: David Brenner sent brownies.

jokes.text: What does the award look like? It's probably a statue of a guy's head halfway up another guy's ass. I already have a fountain like that.
jokes.setup: Larry asks about the award appearance
jokes.punchline: His cynical description escalated by claiming he owns such a fountain
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - starts with cynical Hollywood observation, ends with Larry's twisted taste in décor

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What does the award look like? It's probably a statue of a guy's head halfway up another guy's ass. I already have a fountain like that.

jokes.text: This is it, gentlemen. This is fucking it. The seeds of disrespect for Hank Kingsley that have been sown so liberally around here have finally come to bloom.
jokes.setup: Hank discovers something wrong with his newsletter
jokes.punchline: His overwrought, melodramatic speech about 'seeds of disrespect'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank Kingsley pomposity - treating minor office prank like Shakespearean betrayal

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: This is it, gentlemen. This is fucking it. The seeds of disrespect for Hank Kingsley that have been sown so liberally around here have finally come to bloom.

jokes.text: Doesn't the left one look longer? Absolutely. Can we, uh—Can we? Bring it up about a quarter—
jokes.setup: Hank having his pants fitted
jokes.punchline: His obsession with minor details while ranting about disrespect
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's vanity interrupting his outrage about respect

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Doesn't the left one look longer? Absolutely. Can we, uh—Can we? Bring it up about a quarter—

jokes.text: "Hank's memories"? "I remember those winter days when I'd return home from my paper route, my toes and fingers dead from the cold. How I wanted to quit that job, but whenever I mentioned it to mom, she always said, 'Hank, always remember penis, vagina. Ooh. Penis, vagina. Penis, vagina.'"
jokes.setup: Larry reading from Hank's newsletter
jokes.punchline: The completely inappropriate and nonsensical 'penis, vagina' insertion in childhood memory
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist sabotage - the innocent childhood memory corrupted by crude insertion creates perfect cringe comedy

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: "Hank's memories"? "I remember those winter days when I'd return home from my paper route, my toes and fingers dead from the cold. How I wanted to quit that job, but whenever I mentioned it to mom, she always said, 'Hank, always remember penis, vagina. Ooh. Penis, vagina. Penis, vagina.'"

jokes.text: What the hell is this, Hank? It is unbelievable, isn't it? Yeah. This is what my mom used to say to me. What are the odds of that?
jokes.setup: Larry's incredulous reaction to the newsletter sabotage
jokes.punchline: Hank's complete obliviousness, treating it as remarkable coincidence
jokes.explanation: Perfect character stupidity - Hank's failure to realize he's been pranked makes it even funnier

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What the hell is this, Hank?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It is unbelievable, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: This is what my mom used to say to me.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What are the odds of that?

jokes.text: Paper doesn't grow on trees.
jokes.setup: Arthur complaining about office supply waste
jokes.punchline: His malapropism about paper not growing on trees
jokes.explanation: Classic malapropism - Arthur gets the saying backwards, creating perfect character moment

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: Paper doesn't grow on trees.

jokes.text: So it wasn't you, Lar?
jokes.setup: Hank ranting about finding the newsletter saboteur
jokes.punchline: He casually asks Larry as afterthought using nickname
jokes.explanation: Good character beat - Hank's casual suspicion of Larry shows their dynamic perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: So it wasn't you, Lar?

jokes.text: Well, I guess we know which side of the family you get that mouth from.
jokes.setup: Hank telling Phil to shut his mouth after penis/vagina incident
jokes.punchline: Phil connecting Hank's crude language to the mother's 'advice' from newsletter
jokes.explanation: Smart callback that connects Hank's outburst to the fake mother's advice, showing Phil's wit

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Well, I guess we know which side of the family you get that mouth from.

jokes.text: It screams you. / It screams penis, vagina more than anything.
jokes.setup: Accusation about newsletter sabotage
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation of what the newsletter 'screams'
jokes.explanation: Good callback that takes the 'screams you' metaphor literally. Shows Phil's deadpan wit while advancing the plot.

dialogue.line: It screams you.
It screams penis, vagina more than anything.

jokes.text: I'd have said you come home from a hard day at the paper route. Your mom's there with a thin dressing gown that slips open, and...
jokes.setup: Phil explaining how he would have done the newsletter prank better
jokes.punchline: His disturbing sexual scenario involving Hank's mother
jokes.explanation: Shows Phil's disturbing creativity and willingness to escalate pranks to uncomfortable territory

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I'd have said you come home from a hard day at the paper route. Your mom's there with a thin dressing gown that slips open, and...

jokes.text: My hands are over a barrel.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining why he can't help Steven Wright get the slot
jokes.punchline: His malapropism mixing 'hands are tied' and 'over a barrel'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's tendency to mangle common expressions

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: My hands are over a barrel.

jokes.text: He—that guy has such a weird energy.
jokes.setup: Larry mentions Bobcat Goldthwait as the choice
jokes.punchline: Steven Wright, known for his own weird energy, criticizing someone else's weird energy
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - Steven Wright's deadpan style is itself 'weird energy,' making his criticism hypocritical

dialogue.speaker: Steven Wright
dialogue.line: He—that guy has such a weird energy.

jokes.text: Sure, long enough To say penis, vagina.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking Hank to hang out for a second
jokes.punchline: Hank's bitter callback to his newsletter incident
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Hank's ongoing embarrassment and paranoia about the incident

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Sure, long enough To say penis, vagina.

jokes.text: I gotta run, uh, 'cause I have some toast in the toasterette. [Chuckles] Not the toasterette, the kitchenette.
jokes.setup: Hank meeting Jerry's religious mother after penis/vagina incident
jokes.punchline: His nervous over-correction about appliance names
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Hank's nervousness around Jerry's religious mother after the newsletter incident

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I gotta run, uh, 'cause I have some toast in the toasterette. [Chuckles] Not the toasterette, the kitchenette.

jokes.text: I'll be 111 when I have a kid in little league.
jokes.setup: Jerry discussing wanting a wife eventually
jokes.punchline: His exaggerated calculation of how old he'll be as a father
jokes.explanation: Standard aging comedian anxiety, but the specific number makes it somewhat memorable

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'll be 111 when I have a kid in little league.

jokes.text: Even on a blind date, I insist we go to counseling on the way.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining his relationship anxieties
jokes.punchline: The absurd pre-emptive counseling on first dates
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of dating anxiety to absurd logical extreme - counseling before even meeting

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Even on a blind date, I insist we go to counseling on the way.

jokes.text: Duh! Larry, come on. What are you, crazy? You're joking. I got a series and movies. Besides, who wants to do a talk show five nights a week? You.
jokes.setup: Jerry protesting he doesn't want the late-night slot
jokes.punchline: His final 'You' reveals he knows exactly who wants it
jokes.explanation: Smart reversal - Jerry's protest reveals he understands Larry's desperation perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Duh! Larry, come on. What are you, crazy? You're joking. I got a series and movies. Besides, who wants to do a talk show five nights a week? You.

jokes.text: I would like to accept this very gracious recognition. Thank you very much.
jokes.setup: Larry practicing his awards acceptance speech
jokes.punchline: His pretentious, over-rehearsed delivery for an award he claims not to care about
jokes.explanation: Good character hypocrisy - Larry practicing speech for awards he previously dismissed

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I would like to accept this very gracious recognition. Thank you very much.

jokes.text: The animal... "Ah ah ah!" He shouts. It'll be good going Into commercials-- "We'll be right back!"
jokes.setup: Dave reacting to hearing about Bobcat
jokes.punchline: His impression and practical application of the voice
jokes.explanation: Solid observational comedy about how Bobcat's voice could work in late-night format

dialogue.speaker: David Letterman
dialogue.line: The animal... "Ah ah ah!" He shouts. It'll be good going Into commercials-- "We'll be right back!"

jokes.text: Tom Snyder. But don't tell anyone. Oh, come on. Tom Snyder—tomorrow—Tom Snyder. Come on. No. Yeah. Tom Snyder.
jokes.setup: Letterman finally reveals his secret 12:30 choice
jokes.punchline: The anticlimactic reveal of Tom Snyder, repeated multiple times
jokes.explanation: The repetition and Larry's shocked reaction to the mundane choice creates absurd comedy

dialogue.speaker: Letterman
dialogue.line: Tom Snyder. But don't tell anyone. Oh, come on. Tom Snyder—tomorrow—Tom Snyder. Come on. No. Yeah. Tom Snyder.

jokes.text: Dick Cavett tied up?
jokes.setup: Letterman reveals Tom Snyder as his choice
jokes.punchline: Larry's sarcastic suggestion of another outdated host
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic escalation - implying Letterman is choosing from has-beens

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Dick Cavett tied up?

jokes.text: Take the award. I'm not kidding around this time. Take it, please. It would mean a lot to me to take it. Go ahead. Okay. You're insane! Boy, oh, boy, are you nuts. You're a fucker.
jokes.setup: Letterman offers Larry his award
jokes.punchline: Larry actually takes it, shocking Letterman
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Larry's shameless acceptance of the award reveals his true priorities

dialogue.speaker: Letterman
dialogue.line: Take the award. I'm not kidding around this time. Take it, please. It would mean a lot to me to take it. Go ahead.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Letterman
dialogue.line: You're insane! Boy, oh, boy, are you nuts. You're a fucker.

jokes.text: I won't even have real chairs. I'll have beanbag chairs. I won't even have a desk. I'll have a beanbag desk.
jokes.setup: Bobcat describing his show's unique elements
jokes.punchline: The escalation from beanbag chairs to impossible beanbag desk
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation showing Bobcat's terrible commercial instincts

dialogue.speaker: Bobcat
dialogue.line: I won't even have real chairs. I'll have beanbag chairs. I won't even have a desk. I'll have a beanbag desk.

jokes.text: So a guest comes on, puts a beverage on the wobbly desk. That will be funny, right?
jokes.setup: Bobcat explaining the beanbag desk concept
jokes.punchline: His desperate need for validation about obviously unfunny concept
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy - Bobcat's insecurity about his terrible ideas

dialogue.speaker: Bobcat
dialogue.line: So a guest comes on, puts a beverage on the wobbly desk. That will be funny, right?

jokes.text: Wednesday Is no-pants night. I don't care who The guest is. No pants.
jokes.setup: Bobcat continuing his show concepts
jokes.punchline: The arbitrary and inappropriate clothing rule
jokes.explanation: Absurdist concept that's more random than cleverly constructed

dialogue.speaker: Bobcat
dialogue.line: Wednesday Is no-pants night. I don't care who The guest is. No pants.

jokes.text: I'll have a giant nerf bat, bigger than this. I'll just go, 'That's fascinating. Please tell me some more about your pets.' Boom! Smack her right in the face! I'll hit her so hard, her makeup will stick. It'll be like the holy shroud of Donna.
jokes.setup: Bobcat describing his show's 'regular feature'
jokes.punchline: The violent assault on guests escalated to religious imagery
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation that perfectly shows why Bobcat would destroy the show

dialogue.speaker: Bobcat
dialogue.line: I'll have a giant nerf bat, bigger than this. I'll just go, 'That's fascinating. Please tell me some more about your pets.' Boom! Smack her right in the face! I'll hit her so hard, her makeup will stick. It'll be like the holy shroud of Donna.

jokes.text: I'll hit her so hard, Her makeup will stick. It'll be like The holy shroud of donna.
jokes.setup: Bobcat explaining his nerf bat guest-hitting concept
jokes.punchline: The religious reference to the violent comedy bit
jokes.explanation: Dark but creative escalation with great religious imagery applied to talk show violence

dialogue.speaker: Bobcat
dialogue.line: I'll hit her so hard, Her makeup will stick. It'll be like The holy shroud of donna.

jokes.text: The smothers brothers Are actually brothers. Coulier and saget Aren't related.
jokes.setup: Arthur suggesting pairing Coulier and Saget like the Smothers Brothers
jokes.punchline: Larry's literal correction missing the point
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan moment showing Larry's literal-mindedness in missing Arthur's actual point

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: The smothers brothers Are actually brothers.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Coulier and saget Aren't related.

jokes.text: Then sometimes, I won't even be on the show. My voice will come from a box like this. It will be this disembodied voice, you know, like a Beckett play.
jokes.setup: Bobcat continuing his show pitch
jokes.punchline: His pretentious comparison of hidden-voice gimmick to Beckett
jokes.explanation: Perfect pretentious comedy - comparing lazy TV gimmick to serious literature

dialogue.speaker: Bobcat
dialogue.line: Then sometimes, I won't even be on the show. My voice will come from a box like this. It will be this disembodied voice, you know, like a Beckett play.

jokes.text: Some asshole put a potato in the tailpipe of my car. That's awful. Who would do that? I'll kill the bastard.
jokes.setup: Sid complaining about car sabotage
jokes.punchline: His murderous response to a harmless prank
jokes.explanation: Standard escalation comedy showing Sid's disproportionate anger

dialogue.speaker: Sid
dialogue.line: Some asshole put a potato in the tailpipe of my car. That's awful. Who would do that? I'll kill the bastard.

jokes.text: Bill Clinton nominated a woman to the Supreme Court, or as Clarence Thomas likes to say... New meat.
jokes.setup: Larry's monologue about Supreme Court nomination
jokes.punchline: The inappropriate sexual predator callback to Clarence Thomas
jokes.explanation: Sharp political comedy with good setup/punchline structure typical of late-night monologues

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Bill Clinton nominated a woman to the Supreme Court, or as Clarence Thomas likes to say... New meat.

jokes.text: He's an old friend and a very talented man—Mr. Tom Snyder!
jokes.setup: Larry introducing the 12:30 host choice
jokes.punchline: The complete reversal from Bobcat to the traditional Tom Snyder
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup payoff - complete reversal from Bobcat shows Larry's capitulation to network pressure

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: He's an old friend and a very talented man—Mr. Tom Snyder!

jokes.text: It was worth it. We got him. How did old Bobcat take it? I thought you told Bobcat. You were going to tell him. Someone better tell him before this goes on. He's your pal. It's your idea.
jokes.setup: Arthur and Larry celebrating the Tom Snyder coup
jokes.punchline: The realization that nobody told Bobcat he was dumped
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing the typical Hollywood betrayal and buck-passing

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It was worth it. We got him. How did old Bobcat take it?

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: I thought you told Bobcat.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You were going to tell him.

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: Someone better tell him before this goes on. He's your pal. It's your idea.

jokes.text: When Letterman said he was thinking of Tom Snyder, you're sure he wasn't joking? No. He wasn't joking. Yeah. 'Cause I asked Tom about it, and he never heard about the Letterman thing. Really? Really.
jokes.setup: Arthur questioning Larry's story about Letterman's choice
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Tom Snyder knew nothing about Letterman supposedly choosing him
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending reveal - Larry's entire justification was based on a lie, showing his desperation and dishonesty

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: When Letterman said he was thinking of Tom Snyder, you're sure he wasn't joking?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No. He wasn't joking.

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: Yeah. 'Cause I asked Tom about it, and he never heard about the Letterman thing.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: Really.

tmdb_overview: Everybody has their own ideas about who should host the show being readied to follow Larry, while Larry worries over not winning a major award again.
